title: Having Kids
gist: Paul Graham recounts how his pre-parenthood dread of having kids was driven by selection bias and proved largely wrong, while warning that his post-kids judgment may itself be chemically warped. He concedes the real trade-offs: kids reduce productivity, force schedule-bound work, and may lower ambition because attention is zero-sum.

insights.claim: Strong negative priors about a life experience can be an artifact of selection bias, because one only notices the bad cases (e.g. kids only when they make noise in shared bottlenecks like airplanes), never the quiet good moments.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: When forming a view from observed cases, ask what the silent, unobserved cases look like before trusting a strong prior built only on salient bad examples.
insights.quote: Of course the times I noticed kids were when things were going wrong. I only noticed them when they made noise.
insights.locator: there was a huge amount of selection bias

insights.claim: The good instances of a phenomenon are systematically under-reported because they are quiet and hard to put into words, biasing the available evidence toward the negative.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Treat the absence of testimony about positive cases as a sampling artifact, not evidence the positives don't exist.
insights.quote: What I didn't notice, because they tend to be much quieter, were all the great moments parents had with kids.
insights.locator: What I didn't notice, because they tend to be much quieter

insights.claim: A judgment can be untrustworthy when the very experience being evaluated chemically alters the evaluator, like a convert vouching for a cult only because joining rewired him to be happy in it.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Discount endorsements from people whose preferences were transformed by the thing they are endorsing; the testimony may reflect altered priors, not the underlying value.
insights.quote: To some extent I'm like a religious cultist telling you that you'll be happy if you join the cult too — but only because joining the cult will alter your mind
insights.locator: So to some extent you can't trust me when I say having kids is great

insights.claim: Having kids tends to reduce ambition because only one idea can be the top idea in your mind at a time and attention is a zero-sum game that kids increasingly capture.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Major life commitments compete directly for the scarce top-of-mind slot that drives ambitious work; budget that finite attention deliberately.
insights.quote: attention is a zero-sum game. Only one idea at a time can be the top idea in your mind.
insights.locator: having kids may make one less ambitious

insights.claim: Kids force work onto a fixed schedule rather than letting it spill across all hours, yet Graham finds output stays adequate because work, like love, adapts to whatever times are available.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Constraining work to fixed blocks need not collapse total output; forced scheduling can substitute for unbounded availability.
insights.quote: Work, like love, finds a way. If there are only certain times it can happen, it happens at those times.
insights.locator: I've been able to adapt to working this way

insights.claim: Much of the freedom and optionality people fear losing is never actually exercised, so its loss costs less than anticipated even as it is paid for in loneliness.
insights.speaker: Paul Graham
insights.so_what: Value optionality by how often it is actually used, not by its theoretical existence; unused freedom is an overvalued asset.
insights.quote: most of the freedom I had before kids, I never used. I paid for it in loneliness, but I never used it.
